pass1_reasoning: P2: With the final quest failed on a team containing P2, they need an endgame pivot by over-weighting true-but-minor points (e.g., P1’s earlier failed leadership / prior no-votes) to pull heat onto a good player. P3: As high-Mach evil, they should sound maximally "data-driven" while steering conclusions away from the simple implication of the Round 5 team by candidly recounting facts that subtly blame others. P4: With decisive new evidence (Round 5 fail implies P2 is evil given P1/P5 are good), P4 should stop exploring narratives and plainly lay out the forced logic to lock the table. P5: As the Round 5 leader and a low-GRS good who just presided over the losing proposal, P5 should coherently explain the proposal logic and then align to the clearest deduction (P2) without overcomplicating probabilities.

P4: Low-Mach P4 benefits most from leaving out the most incriminating through-line (that the fail exactly matches the suspected pair) and instead selectively highlight prior passes to muddy certainty without overcommitting. P5: High-Mach P5 should go maximally adversarial by challenging others’ memory/logic around what the Round 5 fail ‘proves,’ attempting to destabilize the emerging consensus and reopen suspicion on P2/P3.

P3: With low GRS and their Round 4 rational-logic anchor challenged by the new FAIL, P3 will default to over-explaining their prior trust-core logic while converging onto suspicion of P4. P4: As the leader on the failing final quest and high Mach-IV, P4 should use strategic omission to avoid addressing the cleanest inference (P4 is locked) while planting doubt about who could have failed earlier. P5: With the game on the line and already positioned as a skeptic of 'confirmed cores,' P5 should use misleading emphasis to spotlight the Round 1 fail with P1 (and universal yes-votes) to muddy certainty and keep P4 from being singled out.

P2: With the decisive fail and their low-GRS style, they’ll try to talk their way clear by re-litigating prior logic in excessive detail rather than cleanly accusing. P5: Under mounting heat from being on the Round 4 fail, low-Mach P5 will stick to opportunistic skewing—reframing the fails as leadership/bloc incompetence rather than player-alignment evidence.

P5: High-GRS good should double down on structured facts from all three fails to force commitment to the P2+P4 solve and reject any last-minute reframes onto the R1/R3 pass participants.
